coredata.dc:title: Metal and heteroatoms co-doped fluorescent carbon dots for highly selective and sensitive detection of Mg2+ ions in aqueous media: Applications in test strips, pharmaceutical, real samples and bioimaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Priyadharshini, Alagarsamy
Napoleon, Ayyakannu Arumugam
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we successfully synthesized fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) through a one-pot hydrothermal method, utilizing tartaric acid, cysteine, and nickel chloride as precursors. This approach allowed for the incorporation of metal ion (nickel) as well as nitrogen and sulfur heteroatoms into the carbon dot structure, resulting in nickel-doped, nitrogen, and sulfur-co-doped CDs (Ni/NS-CDs). A comprehensive characterization of the Ni/NS-CDs was conducted using FT-IR, XRD, Raman, XPS, and HR-TEM analysis confirmed the successful doping of nickel, nitrogen, and sulfur into the Ni/NS-CDs. HR-TEM analysis showed that the synthesized Ni/NS-CDs were spherical with a well-monodispersed average particle size of 2.1 ± 0.2 nm. The Ni/NS-CDs displayed strong fluorescence with excitation-dependent emission behavior and a high quantum yield of 23.60 %. When excited at 480 nm, the Ni/NS-CDs exhibited bright green emission at 524 nm, showcasing their excellent fluorescent characteristics. Additionally, the Ni/NS-CDs demonstrated remarkable stability across a broad pH range and high salt ionic strengths, further enhancing their practical applicability in diverse environments. The Ni/NS-CDs showed high sensitivity and selectivity towards Mg2+ ions, with an impressively low limit of detection (LOD) of 19.38 nM within a wider range of 0–50 µM. Beyond sensing applications, the bright and stable fluorescence of the Ni/NS-CDs enabled clear cell imaging, making them promising candidates for use in biomedical research and diagnostics. Moreover, the practical utility of the Ni/NS-CDs were successfully applied for detecting Mg2+ ions in environmental samples, illustrating their potential for ecological monitoring and water quality assessment. This broad applicability underscores the multifunctionality of the synthesized Ni/NS-CDs, positioning them as valuable nanomaterials in fields such as pharmaceuticals, bio-imaging, environmental science, and analytical chemistry.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Metal-heteroatom
Fluorescence
Bioimaging
Pharmaceutical applications
Environmental sample

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structural and morphological features of Ni/NS-CDs
Selectivity and sensitivity of metal ions
Rapid detection of Mg2+ using test strips

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The Vellore Institute of Technology is recognized for providing the XRD and XPS facilities, and the authors thank VIT University for providing us with research and laboratory facilities, including the SIF-VIT university.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–vis spectrum of Ni/NS-CDs, (b) Fluorescence spectrum of Ni/NS-CDs (λex = 480 nm; λem = 524 nm). Inset: photos captured in natural light and 365 nm UV light), and (c) Fluorescence spectrum of Ni/NS-CDs in excitation varied between 400 nm and 500 nm.
(a and b) Ni/NS-CDs stability of various pH (1–13) and NaCl (0.2–1.0 M). (c and d) The corresponding images of pH (1–13) and NaCl (0.2–1.0 M) under sunlight and UV light at 365 nm.
(a) FT-IR (b) PXRD (c) Raman spectrum of Ni/NS-CDs.
(a) TEM (inset HRTEM) and (d) particle size distribution histogram of Ni/NS-CDs.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of Ni/NS-CDs and (b–f) High-resolution XPS spectra of (b) Ni2p, (c) N1s, (d) S2p, (e) C1s, and (f) O1s, respectively.

(a) FL spectra of Ni/NS-CDs in the presence of various cations. (b) FL spectra of Ni/NS-CDs in the presence Mg2+ ions (0–50 μM) (inset; the corresponding color changes images of Ni/NS-CDs and Ni/NS-CDs/Mg2+ under UV light irradiation at 365 nm), (c) The corresponding color changes images of Ni/NS-CDs in the presence of various cations under daylight and UV light irradiation at 365 nm, (d) Benesi-Hildebrand plot showing the 1/[F − F0] vs. 1/[Mg2+] ion concentrations, and (e) The limit of detection based on calibration curve of Ni/NS-CDs towards Mg2+ ions at emission wavelengths of 524 nm.

(a) Job’s plot analysis of Ni/NS-CDs in different mole fraction of Mg2+ ions concentration 50 μM. (b) Fluorescent spectrum of (I) Ni/NS-CDs, (II) Ni/NS-CDs with Mg2+, and (III) Ni/NS-CDs + Mg2+ + EDTA (inset; the corresponding solution color changes images of Ni/NS-CDs, Ni/NS-CDs/Mg2+ and Ni/NS-CDs/Mg2+ + EDTA).
Color data (RGB values) of the Ni/NS-CDs, Ni/NS-CDs/Mg2+ using a mobile phone application (Color detector, RGB color selector).
(a) Test strip analysis of Ni/NS-CDs in the presence of various metal ions and (b) Fluorescent color changes of Ni/NS-CDs and Ni/NS-CDs + Mg2+ complex.
(a) Fluorescent spectra of Ni/NS-CDs/Mg2+ ions in Gelusil tablets and Gelusil syrup. (b) Fluorescent color changes for the addition of the Gelusil extract to Ni/NS-CDs.
Bright-field, Fluorescence, and merged confocal images of Ni/NS-CDs and Ni/NS-CDs towards Mg2+ in HCT-116 cells.
Schematic illustration of the synthesis of Ni/NS-CDs.
Determination of Mg2+ ions in different water samples using the proposed method.

head.ce:title: Metal and heteroatoms co-doped fluorescent carbon dots for highly selective and sensitive detection of Mg2+ ions in aqueous media: Applications in test strips, pharmaceutical, real samples and bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, School of Advanced Sciences, Vellore Institute of Technology, Vellore 632014, Tamil Nadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, School of Advanced Sciences, Vellore Institute of Technology, Vellore 632014, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we successfully synthesized fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) through a one-pot hydrothermal method, utilizing tartaric acid, cysteine, and nickel chloride as precursors. This approach allowed for the incorporation of metal ion (nickel) as well as nitrogen and sulfur heteroatoms into the carbon dot structure, resulting in nickel-doped, nitrogen, and sulfur-co-doped CDs (Ni/NS-CDs). A comprehensive characterization of the Ni/NS-CDs was conducted using FT-IR, XRD, Raman, XPS, and HR-TEM analysis confirmed the successful doping of nickel, nitrogen, and sulfur into the Ni/NS-CDs. HR-TEM analysis showed that the synthesized Ni/NS-CDs were spherical with a well-monodispersed average particle size of 2.1 ± 0.2 nm. The Ni/NS-CDs displayed strong fluorescence with excitation-dependent emission behavior and a high quantum yield of 23.60 %. When excited at 480 nm, the Ni/NS-CDs exhibited bright green emission at 524 nm, showcasing their excellent fluorescent characteristics. Additionally, the Ni/NS-CDs demonstrated remarkable stability across a broad pH range and high salt ionic strengths, further enhancing their practical applicability in diverse environments. The Ni/NS-CDs showed high sensitivity and selectivity towards Mg2+ ions, with an impressively low limit of detection (LOD) of 19.38 nM within a wider range of 0–50 µM. Beyond sensing applications, the bright and stable fluorescence of the Ni/NS-CDs enabled clear cell imaging, making them promising candidates for use in biomedical research and diagnostics. Moreover, the practical utility of the Ni/NS-CDs were successfully applied for detecting Mg2+ ions in environmental samples, illustrating their potential for ecological monitoring and water quality assessment. This broad applicability underscores the multifunctionality of the synthesized Ni/NS-CDs, positioning them as valuable nanomaterials in fields such as pharmaceuticals, bio-imaging, environmental science, and analytical chemistry.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a novel class of carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials with unique properties such as strong fluorescence, excellent biocompatibility, affordability, and a variety of synthesis methods [1]. These nanoparticles are typically smaller than 10 nm. Since their serendipitous discovery in 2004 during electrophoretic fractionation, fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted considerable attention, being classified into two main types: carbon dots and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [2]. Research has increasingly focused on expanding the functional applications of CDs for purposes such as drug delivery, sensing, and bioimaging. Currently, CDs are primarily synthesized using two techniques: top-down and bottom-up approaches. The top-down method involves breaking down larger carbon structures into nanoparticles through physical and chemical processes like laser ablation [3], electrochemical synthesis [4], and arc discharge [5]. The bottom-up approach uses chemical methods to synthesize small carbon molecules larger nanoparticles, employing techniques like microwave-assisted synthesis [6], hydrothermal/solvothermal processes [7], template methods [8], chemical oxidation [9], and combustion [10] are commonly employed in this approach.

The core of carbon dots (CDs) is often composed of crystalline sp2-hybridized carbon, while their surfaces are rich in functional groups such as carboxyl, amino, and hydroxyl groups [11–13]. Recent research on the fluorescence mechanism of carbon quantum dots identifies surface functionalization and heteroatom doping as effective strategies to enhance fluorescence and expand applications [14–16]. CDs produced via the hydrothermal/solvothermal method typically show blue or green fluorescence in the shorter-wavelength region but are valued for their simplicity and efficiency [17,18]. To develop CDs with long-wavelength fluorescence, significant progress has been made, particularly through heteroatom doping [19]. Heteroatoms significantly alter CDs electronic structure, nanostructure, and composition by modifying electron distribution through the push–pull effect and orbital overlap with carbon [20]. The incorporation of non-metallic atoms influences the overall electrical distribution and electronic energy levels of CDs. In particular, non-metallic doping-such as single-atom doping with nitrogen (N) [21], sulfur (S) [22], phosphorus (P) [23], boron (B) [24], and fluorine (F) [25] as well as co-doping with two elements like N/S [26], N/P [27], F/N [28], or multiple elements like N/S/B [29], has been widely researched and proven effective in tailoring the fluorescence and other properties of CDs.

Metal ions, with more electrons, larger radii, and empty orbitals, modify CDs electron density, energy gap, and properties upon doping [30,31]. Research on metal ion doping-using elements like manganese (Mn), nickel (Ni), copper (Cu), cobalt (Co), zinc (Zn), magnesium (Mg), and iron (Fe) has progressed rapidly. For example [32], synthesized manganese-doped CDs (Mn-CDs) based on pyromellitic acid, which exhibited dual fluorescence emissions [33] reported a rapid microwave-assisted method to prepare ethylenediamine-customized CDs doped with Mn2+, Fe2+, Co2+, and Ni2+, significantly enhancing FLQY. Additionally [34], developed Cu, N-co-doped CDs (Cu, N-CDs) for detecting metabolites in H2O2-generation processes. Nickel, chosen for its non-toxicity and surface reactivity, improves CDs structural and optical properties. To enhance quantum yield (QY), S and N co-doping were applied, boosting fluorescence efficiency and electronic properties. Notably, Ni-doped and N, S-co-doped CDs (Ni/NS-CDs) remain unreported, with no studies on their analytical, biomedical applications, or biological evaluation as fluorescent materials. This highlights the need for high-QY Ni/NS-CDs to explore their fluorescence properties and potential uses in sensing and functional applications.

In this work, we synthesized Ni/NS-CDs for the first time using tartaric acid, nickel chloride, and cysteine as precursors (Scheme 1 ). This novel synthesis approach allows for the incorporation of nickel as a doping agent, while nitrogen and sulfur co-doping provides additional electronic and structural benefits, contributing to the fluorescence enhancement of the resulting Ni/NS-CDs. The optical and structural properties of Ni/NS-CDs were comprehensively characterized, revealing their potential in bioimaging, sensing, and environmental monitoring applications. UV–Vis absorption spectra showed a peak at 270 nm, corresponding to the π-–π* transition in sp2 carbon domains, while a broader band at 450 nm indicated surface defects introduced by Ni, N, and S doping, enhancing their optical behavior. Fluorescence measurements demonstrated strong green emission at 524 nm, with a quantum yield of 23.6 %, making Ni/NS-CDs suitable for fluorescence sensing. Stability studies across pH and ionic strength conditions confirmed the robustness of the fluorescence properties. Advanced techniques like FT-IR, XRD, and XPS revealed the successful doping of Ni, N, and S into the CDs, with key functional groups like OH, CO, SH, and NH playing roles in tuning their fluorescence activities. The selectivity of Ni/NS-CDs toward Mg2+ ions was particularly striking, with significant fluorescence enhancement and a low detection limit of 19.38 nM, alongside a reversible fluorescence response. Practical applications were demonstrated in detecting Mg2+ ions in pharmaceutical samples, water quality analysis, and live cell imaging, where Ni/NS-CDs showed promising fluorescence intensity changes, especially in the presence of Mg2+ ions. Additionally, smartphone-based RGB analysis and test strips highlighted the sensor practicality for rapid and on-site detection of Mg2+ ions, further underscoring its potential in environmental and healthcare diagnostics.

Tartaric acid and nickel chloride were purchased from Avra Synthesis Pvt. Ltd. L-cysteine was bought from Harish Laboratories Pvt. Ltd. Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and acetonitrile (ACN) were purchased from High Purity Laboratory Chemicals Pvt. Ltd. The water used in all experiments was purified using a Millipore filtration system to ensure high purity and eliminate any potential contaminants.

The hydrothermal method is commonly used for synthesizing metal-doped and heteroatom co-doped carbon dots (CDs), such as nickel-doped and nitrogen-sulfur co-doped CDs (Ni/NS-CDs) [34]. Briefly, 1.5 g of tartaric acid, 0.65 g of nickel chloride, and 1.2 g of L-cysteine are dissolved in 50 mL of distilled water. This homogeneous solution is then transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 8 h in an oven. After cooling to room temperature, the brown solution is centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 3 h. The supernatant is filtered and subjected to freeze-drying, yielding Ni/NS-CDs. These CDs are subsequently characterized using various techniques, including UV, FL, FT-IR, Raman, XRD, XPS, and HR-TEM techniques.

For optical characterization, UV–visible absorbance spectra were recorded with a HITACHI V-730 spectrometer, while fluorescence emission was measured using a JASCO FP 8200 spectrophotometer. Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was performed with a SHIMADZU IRAffinity-1 spectrophotometer to identify functional groups and chemical bonds within the sample. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was conducted using a Bruker X8 Apex diffractometer to determine the crystalline structure of the Ni/NS-CDs. Raman spectroscopy was performed to investigate the SERS behaviors of substrates using a Raman (Horiba HR Evolution 800) spectrometer with 532 nm laser wavelength, 48 mW laser power, 1200 gr/mm diffraction grid, and 500 nm spatial resolution at the core research support center for natural products and medical materials. Surface and core compositions were investigated through X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) with a Thermo Scientific Multilab-2000 spectrometer, which utilizes an Al Kα monochromatized source. To prepare samples for high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) imaging, an aqueous solution of Ni/NS-CDs was deposited onto a carbon-coated copper grid and allowed to dry at room temperature. The dried grid was then examined using the JEOL JEM-2100F high-resolution transmission electron microscope at 200 kV.

In order to maintain the absorption intensity less than 0.1, the excitation wavelength was adjusted to 480 nm and the Ni/NS-CDs solution was appropriately diluted. Then, solutions for measurements of absorption and emission were placed inside a 1 × 1 cm quartz cuvette. Quantum yield (QY) was assessed using a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The fluorescence quantum yield (FLQY) can be computed using the following formula:
ϕ x
=
ϕ
reference
×
A
reference
A
sample
×
I
sample
I
reference
×
ƞ
sample
ƞ
reference
2
Here, ϕx represents the quantum yield, while ϕ reference denotes the quantum yield of the sample and reference of the Ni/NS-CDs. A referenc
e and A sample correspond to the absorbance of the reference and sample of the Ni/NS-CDs, respectively, while I sampl
e and I referenc
e represent the areas of the sample and reference of the Ni/NS-CDs. The refractive indices of the Ni/NS-CDs reference and sample are shown by the symbols ηsample and ηreference, respectively. The fluorescence quantum yield was documented as 23.60 %.

To evaluate the selectivity of Ni/NS-CDs towards different metal ions, a comprehensive study was conducted involving various ions including Al3+, Ba2+, Ce3+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Sr2+, Mg2+, Zr2+, Ag+, and, Zn2+. In this study, 3 mL quartz cuvettes were used to mix solutions of metal ions (1.975 mL at a concentration of 50 μM) with Ni/NS-CDs (25 μL at a concentration of 0.1 μg/mL). Additionally, to investigate the interaction between Ni/NS-CDs and Mg2+ ions, solutions containing varying concentrations of Mg2+ (ranging from 0 to 50 μM) were prepared and combined with the Ni/NS-CDs solution. This was done at room temperature to assess whether the presence of Mg2+ affects the fluorescence enhancement properties of Ni/NS-CDs. The fluorescence emission spectra were recorded using an excitation wavelength of 480 nm, and emission was measured over the range of 500 to 600 nm. This allowed for detailed analysis of the fluorescence response of Ni/NS-CDs in the presence of different metal ions and under varying conditions of Mg2+ concentration.

Water samples collected from diverse sources, including tap water, seawater, and well water, were analyzed for contamination with magnesium (Mg2+) ions. Initially, these samples were filtered using Whatman filter paper to remove any solid impurities and particulates. Following filtration, the samples were spiked with magnesium ions at concentrations ranging from 10 μM to 50 μM. After the addition of Mg2+ ions, the samples were allowed to stand at room temperature for 2 min to ensure thorough mixing and equilibration. Subsequently, the fluorescence (FL) intensities of the samples were measured to assess the presence and concentration of Mg2+ ions. This measurement was performed after the addition of standard Mg2+ solutions to calibrate and compare the fluorescence response.

The potential cytotoxic effects of Ni/NS-CDs and Ni/NS-CDs/Mg2+ on HCT-116 cells were assessed using the MTT assay. Initially, 1 × 105 cells/mL were seeded in a 96-well plate and incubated for 24 h in a humidified atmosphere with 5 % CO2. Subsequently, cells were treated with varying concentrations (100 µg/ml) of Ni/NS-CDs and Ni/NS-CDs/Mg2+ at 37 °C for 24 h. Following treatment, 100 μL of MTT solution (5 mg/10 mL) was added to each well, and incubation continued for another 24 h at 37 °C in 5 % CO2. The media was then removed, and the formed purple-colored formazan was dissolved in 100 μL DMSO. Cell viability measurements were performed in triplicate. HCT-116 cells were cultured on glass coverslips in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) supplemented with fetal bovine serum (FBS) in a humidified atmosphere with 5 % CO2. Cells were then incubated with 25.0 µg/mL of Ni/NS-CDs and Ni/NS-CDs/Mg2+ for 24 h. To evaluate Mg2+ uptake, Ni/NS-CDs-pretreated HCT-116 cells were further incubated with Mg2+ for 4 h at 37 °C. Absorbance measurements were taken at 590 nm using a microplate reader. Cellular uptake and imaging studies were conducted using confocal laser scanning microscopy.

To achieve optimal stability performance, experimental conditions such as pH, and ionic strength were meticulously optimized. The stability of Ni/NS-CDs was evaluated across a broad pH range (1.0–13.0) (Fig. 2a). Notably, the fluorescence (FL) intensity improved significantly as the pH increased from 1.0 to 7.0, whereas further pH increments (8.0–13.0) led to a decrease in FL intensity. As the pH increased from 3 to 9, the Ni/NS-CDs exhibited a gradual shift towards more negative surface charges. This phenomenon was attributed to surface charge modifications resulting from protonation and deprotonation processes. Moreover, the aqueous Ni/NS-CDs solution demonstrated exceptional salt resistance, showcasing its potential for applications in diverse environments. Furthermore, the effects of electrolyte concentration on the fluorescence properties of Ni/NS-CDs were investigated. Solutions of Ni/NS-CDs were prepared with NaCl molarities ranging from 0.2 M to 1.0 M (Fig. 2b). Notably, despite the variations in NaCl concentration, the fluorescence intensity showed minimal changes, indicating that the fluorescence characteristics of Ni/NS-CDs are robust against alterations in ionic strength. This stability under varying environmental conditions is advantageous for potential applications, as it ensures consistent fluorescence performance. To further explore the optical characteristics of Ni/NS-CDs at 365 nm was measured under both daylight and UV illumination to identify any changes in the emission spectrum or intensity (Fig. 2c and 2d). This assessment likely involved spectroscopic analysis or visual inspection [35]. These investigations are crucial for confirming the reliability and practical applicability of Ni/NS-CDs in sensing applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and morphological features of Ni/NS-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The structural characteristics of Ni/NS-CDs were comprehensively analysed using several advanced characterization techniques, including Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction (XRD), Raman spectroscopy, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). These methods allowed for a detailed examination of the morphology, size distribution, surface chemistry, and crystalline structure of the Ni/NS-CDs. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was employed to identify the functional groups present on the surface of the Ni/NS-CDs (Fig. 3 a). The broad absorption band around 3362 cm−1 corresponds to the OH stretching vibrations, indicating the presence of hydroxyl groups on the surface. Peaks at 3310 cm−1 were attributed to the NH stretching, confirming the inclusion of nitrogen in the structure. Additional peaks were observed at 2949 cm−1, corresponding to CH stretching vibrations, suggesting the presence of alkyl chains. A distinct peak at 2649 cm−1 was assigned to the SH stretching, indicating the successful incorporation of sulfur into the carbon dots. Peaks at 1714 cm−1 and 1449 cm−1 were attributed to CO and CN stretching vibrations, further confirming the presence of carbonyl and nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface. Moreover, peaks at 1520 cm−1 and 1213 cm−1 were attributed to CC and CSC stretching in aromatic rings, indicating the existence of benzene-like structures within the carbon dots. Notably, in the metal-sensitive region between 400–600 cm−1, a peak at 528 cm−1 was observed, corresponding to the ONiO stretching vibrations, confirming the successful doping of nickel into the Ni/NS-CDs [36]. These results, taken together, demonstrate the complex surface chemistry of the Ni/NS-CDs, with a variety of functional groups including OH, CH, CO, and SH, which are crucial for tuning the optical and catalytic properties of the carbon dots.

The XRD pattern of Ni/NS-CDs (Fig. 3b) stands out for its remarkable complexity, showcasing sharp, narrow peaks that unequivocally indicate high crystallinity. The patterns complexity is underscored by the presence many peaks with a signal-to-noise ratio greater than 3, confirming the polycrystalline nature of Ni/NS-CDs. The most intense peak, occurring at 2θ = 24.2°, corresponds to a d-spacing of 0.37 nm (002 lattice plane), while the second-most intense peak, at 15.8°, exhibits a d-spacing of 0.56 nm (101 lattice plane) precisely half the value of the most intense peak [37]. This harmonious spacing suggests that the lattice interactions are not randomly determined, but rather facilitated by linkers. The abundance of small peaks scattered throughout the pattern reveals minor variations, potentially yielding similar yet distinct structural configurations. The presence of numerous small peaks indicates a rich structural diversity, with subtle differences likely arising from variations in linker orientation, bonding, or lattice defects. This intricate structural landscape underscores the unique characteristics of Ni/NS-CDs.

The Raman spectrum exhibited one broad peak and two sharp peaks at approximately 1345.05 cm−1 and 1582.21 cm−1, corresponding to the D and G bands, respectively, which are associated with sp3 and sp2 hybridized carbon atoms (Fig. 3c). These bands arise due to the vibrations of sp3 and sp2 hybridized carbon atoms in the hexagonal planes and the two-dimensional terminal faces of disordered graphite structures. The relative intensity ratio of the D and G bands (ID/IG) was calculated to be 0.93, confirming the graphitic nature of the nickel doped with nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (Ni/NS-CDs).

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) was employed to precisely examine the particle size and surface morphology of the Ni/NS-CDs (Fig. 4 a). The analysis revealed that the Ni/NS-CDs were uniformly distributed in an aqueous solution, exhibiting a spherical morphology with diameters ranging from 1 to 6 nm. This even distribution is important for potential applications in bioimaging and drug delivery, where consistent particle size ensures reproducibility and functionality. The HR-TEM images also provided insight into the crystalline nature of the Ni/NS-CDs. Specifically, a lattice spacing of 0.23 nm was observed (inset Fig. 4a), corresponding to the in-plane lattice spacing of graphitic (sp2) carbon [38]. This lattice arrangement confirms the presence of graphitic domains within the carbon dots, which are known to enhance electronic and optical properties. The average size of the Ni/NS-CDs, calculated from the statistical analysis of 100 particles, was found to be 2.1 ± 0.2 nm (Fig. 4b), indicating a narrow size distribution and well-defined nanostructure.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed to analyze the elemental composition and chemical states of the atoms in the Ni/NS-CDs (Fig. 5 ). The XPS spectra revealed prominent peaks for Ni2p at 862 eV, N1s at 400 eV, S2p at 162 eV, C1s at 284 eV, and O1s at 532 eV, indicating the presence of nickel, nitrogen, sulfur, carbon, and oxygen, respectively (Fig. 5a). The elemental composition of the Ni/NS-CDs was quantified as 6.23 % nickel, 16.08 % nitrogen, 12.17 % sulfur, 48.43 % carbon, and 17.09 % oxygen, confirming successful doping with N, S, and Ni. For the Ni2p spectrum (Fig. 5b), the peaks at 855.12 and 864.63 eV could be assigned to Ni2p3/2 and Ni2p1/2 respectively, revealing the presence of Ni in the Ni/NS-CDs [39]. One satellite peak at 860.95 eV in the Ni2p spectrum suggested the presence of a trace amount of nickel oxide due to the exposure of Ni/NS-CDs under the air. Further analysis of the N1s spectrum (Fig. 5c) revealed peaks at 399.1 eV, 399.8 eV, and 401.4 eV, corresponding to pyridine N, pyrrole N, and N–C bonds, respectively.

This indicates the presence of different nitrogen bonding configurations, which can significantly influence the electronic and optical properties of the carbon dots. The S2p spectrum (Fig. 5d) displayed three distinct peaks at 165.7 eV, 166.5 eV, and 167.1 eV, corresponding to S2p3/2, S2p1/2, and SO bonds, respectively. These peaks suggest that sulfur is incorporated in sulfonic acid-type structures, contributing to the surface functionalization of the CDs. The C1s spectrum (Fig. 5e) revealed peaks at 282.2 eV, 283.5 eV, 286.3 eV, and 287.6 eV, attributed to sp2 and sp3 carbon, indicating the presence of CC, CC, CO/CS, and CO bonds. Finally, the O1s spectrum (Fig. 5f) exhibited peaks at 533.1 eV, 533.5 eV, and 534.2 eV, corresponding to CO, CO, and OCO bonds, respectively. These results collectively demonstrate the successful synthesis of Ni-doped, N and S codoped with abundant surface functional groups. These XPS findings, combined with the FTIR results, provide strong evidence of the successful doping of nickel, nitrogen, and sulfur into the carbon dots, resulting in a rich surface chemistry with a variety of functional groups, such as OH, SO, and NH2. These groups are critical for enhancing the fluorescence, catalytic, and sensing capabilities of the Ni/NS-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity of metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence responses of Ni/NS-CDs were accurately examined against a variety of metal ions, including Al3+, Ba2+, Ce3+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Sr2+, Mg2+, Zr2+, Ag+, and, Zn2+. As illustrated in Fig. 6 a, only Mg2+ elicited significant fluorescence changes, highlighting the high selectivity of Ni/NS-CDs for this specific ion. Upon the addition of Mg2+, the fluorescence spectrum showed a notable enhancement, with a significant shift from 520 nm to 540 nm and a pronounced increase in the emission peak at 524 nm. The inset of Fig. 6b further illustrates fluorescence sensing for Mg2+ ions detection beyond metal ion detection, fluorescence sensing can also be leveraged for Mg2+ ion detection. Notably, the addition of Mg2+ ion triggered a significant increase in FL intensity. The FL intensity of Ni/NS-CDs at 524 nm exhibited a gradual enhancement with increasing Mg2+ ions concentrations, indicating the potential of Ni/NS-CDs as a FL probe for Mg2+ ion detection. This concentration dependent response was visually evident, as the green emission of Ni/NS-CDs transformed into a bright green emission upon Mg2+ ion addition, as depicted in the inset photographs (Fig. 6b). The fluorescence color change in Ni/NS-CDs solutions when exposed to various metal ions under 365 nm UV light, reinforcing the capability of Ni/NS-CDs to effectively sense metal ions through their unique fluorescence characteristics. Quantitative detection of Mg2+ ions using Ni/NS-CDs was thoroughly investigated under optimized conditions. Fig. 6c displays visual changes in an aqueous solution of Ni/NS-CDs with and without Mg2+ ions under 365 nm UV light. The fluorescence intensity transitioned from light green to bright green upon the addition of Mg2+, demonstrating a significant enhancement in fluorescence. This enhancement was correlated with the concentration of Mg2+, exhibiting an exponential increase in fluorescence intensity when excited at 480 nm. The phenomenon is attributed to charge transfer between the Ni/NS-CDs and Mg2+ ions, with the observed redshift in emission wavelength linked to coordination between Mg2+ ions and the functional groups on the Ni/NS-CDs surface. A plot of the change in fluorescence (FL) intensity at 524 nm revealed an enhanced efficiency, [1/F-F0], which exhibited a strong linear correlation (R2 = 0.9968) with Mg2+ ions concentrations ranging from 0 to 50 μM (Fig. 6d). This linear relationship enabled quantitative detection of Mg2+ ion. Notably, the limit of detection (LOD) for Mg2+ was calculated to be 19.38 nM, using the equation LOD = 3 × SD/S (Fig. 6e). This value indicates that Ni/NS-CDs can detect Mg2+ with high sensitivity and low detection limits in aqueous media. Further investigations aimed to elucidate the fluorescence-enhancing mechanism for Mg2+ sensing. Although the precise mechanism remains under study, the interaction between Mg2+ ions and the functional groups on the Ni/NS-CDs surface is central to the enhancement process.

To investigate the binding ratio between Ni/NS-CDs and Mg2+, a Job's plot analysis was employed (Fig. 7 a). The fluorescence (FL) intensity at 524 nm was plotted against the molar ratio of [Mg2+] to [Mg2++Ni/NS-CDs]. The results showed that the maximum FL intensity consistently occurred at a molar ratio of 0.5. Job’s plot analysis confirmed a 1:1 stoichiometric ratio between Ni/NS-CDs and Mg2+ ions, supporting the formation of a stable complex. The limit of detection (LOD) was determined to be 19.38 nM, indicating a linear working range of 0–50 μM. These findings suggest promising applications for Ni/NS-CDs in the detection of Mg2+ ions, potentially addressing regulatory standards for drinking water quality set by organizations such as the United States Environmental Protection Agency (USEPA). To verify the effectiveness of the proposed displacement strategy, the fluorescence (FL) behavior of the Ni/NS-CDs/Mg2+ complex was investigated in the presence of various metal ions (Al3+, Ba2+, Ce3+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Sr2+, Zr2+, Ag+, and, Zn2+). Fig. S1 illustrates the upon adding 50 μM Mg2+ with the 50 μM addition of other distinct metal ions placed in 2 min and the interference was tested. No any other changes of interference study upon the addition of other metal ions in Ni/NS-CDs/Mg2+ complex. Moreover, these interference study confirmed by highly selectivity of only Mg2+ ions.

Additionally, the reversibility of the Ni/NS-CDs probe toward Mg2+ ions was demonstrated in a HEPES buffer solution. The use of ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) facilitated the regeneration of free Ni/NS-CDs through the demetallation of the Mg2+ ion complex. The fluorescence of Ni/NS-CDs can be effectively restored using an EDTA solution, as illustrated in Fig. 7b. This restoration process demonstrates the reversibility of the Ni/NS-CDs fluorescence during three titration cycles [40,41]. As shown in the experiments, the addition of Mg2+ significantly enhances the fluorescence intensity of the Ni/NS-CDs. Subsequently, introducing EDTA leads to the recovery of this fluorescence, illustrating a sequential pattern of alternating amplification and quenching. Specifically, after the initial fluorescence enhancement due to Mg2+, the fluorescence intensity can be restored upon the addition of EDTA, effectively reversing the quenching effect of the EDTA-saturated solution. This switching procedure can be repeated three times without any noticeable decline in fluorescence efficiency, with more than 95 % of the fluorescence intensity being recoverable. This finding highlights the robust nature of Ni/NS-CDs as a reliable fluorescent sensor, capable of enduring multiple cycles of enhancement and restoration, which is promising for practical applications in environmental sensing and monitoring.

Smartphones are increasingly favored for analyte detection through digital image analysis of chromogenic responses, offering a straightforward method for data gathering and examination. Color detectors, particularly those integrated into smartphones, provide a feasible and reliable way to measure color changes and detect trace levels of analytes [27]. Smartphones utilize RGB color pickers that analyze colors based on RGB data, with each color channel represented by 8-bit resolution. In image files, colors are depicted through varying pixel intensities of red (R), green (G), and blue (B), with values ranging from 0 (darkest) to 255 (brightest). In an experiment involving a Vivo Y1907 AI triple camera, images of both Ni/NS-CDs and Ni/NS-CDs/Mg2+ aqueous solutions were captured under UV light at 365 nm. An RGB color selector application was then employed to quantify the RGB values of these solutions (Fig. 8 ). The results showed that the Ni/NS-CDs solution appeared light green, with RGB values of (4, 103, 75), while the Ni/NS-CDs/Mg2+ solution displayed a bright green hue, with RGB values of (95, 253, 136). This approach allows for straightforward and immediate onsite visual analysis, enabling users to determine the RGB values of the solutions using color picker applications. Such technology enhances the accessibility and efficiency of colorimetric assays in various fields, including environmental monitoring and healthcare diagnostics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rapid detection of Mg2+ using test strips
ce:section.ce:para: We conducted experiments with test kits utilizing Whatman filter paper to explore the potential applications of the chemosensor probe Ni/NS-CDs [42]. Various metal cation solutions were prepared alongside a stock solution of Ni/NS-CDs. To perform the tests, paper strips were immersed in the Ni/NS-CDs solution and subsequently loaded with room-temperature analytes, including Al3+, Ba2+, Ce3+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Sr2+, Mg2+, Zr2+, Ag+, and, Zn2+. When exposed to UV light at 365 nm, the Ni/NS-CDs-coated strips fluoresced a luminous green color (Fig. 9 a).

Notably, the addition of various cations did not produce significant color changes; however, the introduction of Mg2+ resulted in a striking ratiometric color changes from light green to bright green (Fig. 9b). This clear and immediate response indicates that the Ni/NS-CDs are highly effective in sensitively detecting Mg2+ ions. This straightforward detection method demonstrates the potential for developing rapid and reliable test strips for Mg2+ ion analysis in various settings, making it a promising tool for environmental monitoring and quality control.

The magnesium ion content in antacid pills and syrups was analyzed using the Ni/NS-CDs as a chemosensor[43]. Specifically, we investigated the Mg2+ concentration in pharmaceutical samples, including Gelusil syrup and tablets. For the analysis, 100 mg of a freshly crushed Gelusil tablet (Apollo Pharmaceutical Private Ltd., India) was dissolved in 10 mL of double-distilled water. For the syrup, 1 mL of Gelusil syrup was also diluted in 10 mL of double-distilled water. Subsequently, 100 µl of this syrup solution was added to the Ni/NS-CDs solution. Fluorescence spectroscopy was employed to analyze the resulting solution, revealing new emission peaks at 527 nm and 530 nm (Fig. 10 a). This spectral change was accompanied by a visible color shift in the Ni/NS-CDs from light green to bright green upon the addition of the pharmaceutical samples (Fig. 10b). These findings demonstrate that the Ni/NS-CDs chemosensor can effectively detect and quantify Mg2+ ions in pharmaceutical formulations. This method offers a reliable approach for assessing magnesium content in products like Gelusil syrup and tablets, highlighting its potential for quality control in the pharmaceutical industry.

The application of the developed chemosensor, Ni/NS-CDs, was effectively demonstrated by detecting Mg2+ ions in various water samples, including tap, sea, and well water [44]. The fluorescence (FL) responses were measured without any prior treatment of the samples, utilizing different concentrations of Mg2+ ions (10, 30, and 50 μM) to evaluate the sensor performance. As shown in Table 1 , the recovery rates for Mg2+ ions in these samples ranged from 99.9 % to 101.7 %, indicating excellent accuracy in detection. Furthermore, the relative standard deviation (RSD) for each sample was less than 2.37 %, which reflects outstanding precision and consistency in the measurements. These results underscore the reliability and effectiveness of the label-free Ni/NS-CDs probe for monitoring Mg2+ ions across various real water samples. Importantly, the Mg2+ concentrations measured by the Ni/NS-CDs sensor closely aligned with those obtained through conventional detection methods, reinforcing its potential for practical applications in water quality assessment.

To explore the potential of Ni/NS-CDs in biological applications, bioimaging and cell viability assays were conducted. The MTT assay was employed to evaluate the cytotoxicity of Ni/NS-CDs in HCT-116 cells (Fig. S2). A series concentration (100 μg/mL) of Ni/NS-CDs were incubated with cells for 24 h. Results showed minimal cytotoxicity, with over 94 % cell viability. Confocal laser scanning microscopy was used to investigate the bioimaging capabilities of Ni/NS-CDs. HCT-116 cells were incubated with Ni/NS-CDs (25 μg/mL) for 15 min, rinsed, and examined. Free Ni/NS-CDs exhibited strong fluorescence signals in live cells. For intracellular detection, cells were pre-treated with various metal ions before adding the Ni/NS-CDs. The results indicated a significant enhancement in fluorescence intensity in the presence of Mg2+ ions (Fig. 11 ). Cells exposed solely to the Ni/NS-CDs exhibited a green fluorescence, while those pre-treated with Mg2+ displayed a striking bright green fluorescence. Overlaying phase-contrast images revealed that Ni/NS-CDs exhibited high membrane permeability, with fluorescence signals observed in the intracellular space. These findings suggest that Ni/NS-CDs can effectively serve as a tool for detecting metal ions, particularly Mg2+, within live cells. The results also imply that both the Ni/NS-CDs and metal ions can penetrate the intracellular environment. However, further research is necessary to quantitatively assess metal ion concentrations within the cells. This would provide a deeper understanding of the interactions between metal ions and cellular components. Overall, this study represents a significant advancement in evaluating the potential of Ni/NS-CDs for cellular research and enhances our understanding of metal ion dynamics in living cells.

In summary, we successfully synthesized nickel-doped and nitrogen and sulfur-codoped carbon dots (Ni/NS-CDs) using a straightforward hydrothermal method, employing cysteine as a nitrogen and sulfur source along with nickel chloride as the nickel precursor. The resulting Ni/NS-CDs exhibited remarkable fluorescence properties, achieving a high quantum yield of 23.60 %. Additionally, these carbon dots demonstrated low toxicity, photostability, and biocompatibility. Characterization through FT-IR, XRD, Raman, HR-TEM, and XPS analyses confirmed the incorporation of various heteroatoms (N and S) on the surface of the Ni/NS-CDs. These green-emitting carbon dots were selectively and sensitively utilized for the detection of Mg2+ ions, achieving a limit of detection (LOD) of 19.38 nM. The stoichiometric ratio of the Ni/NS-CDs/Mg2+ complex was determined to be 1:1 using the Job’s plot method, indicating that a single Mg2+ ion binds to one Ni/NS-CD molecule. The stability of the fluorescence under varying ionic strengths and the ability to restore fluorescence upon treatment [41] with EDTA confirm the practical viability of Ni/NS-CDs as sensors. The synthesized Ni/NS-CDs exhibit promising optical properties, characterized by strong fluorescence and selective sensitivity toward Mg2+ ions, making them suitable for various applications in bioimaging and environmental sensing. The enhanced fluorescence response of Ni/NS-CDs to Mg2+ ions support their application in fluorescent cell imaging, specifically in HCT-116 cells, showcasing excellent cell membrane penetrability. Notably, successful detection of Mg2+ in real water samples demonstrates their accuracy and potential for quality control in environmental monitoring. Overall, these findings position Ni/NS-CDs as a versatile and effective tool for both analytical and biomedical applications, warranting further exploration of their functionalities in complex biological systems.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The Vellore Institute of Technology is recognized for providing the XRD and XPS facilities, and the authors thank VIT University for providing us with research and laboratory facilities, including the SIF-VIT university.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2025.125851.
